provisions.title: FSB NBFI Monitoring: Narrow Measure
provisions.text: The FSB monitors non-bank financial intermediation (NBFI) using a narrow measure that focuses on entities engaged in credit intermediation outside the regular banking system. The narrow measure classifies NBFI activities into five economic functions (EFs): EF1 (collective investment vehicles with features that make them susceptible to runs); EF2 (lending dependent on short-term funding); EF3 (intermediation of market activities dependent on short-term funding or secured funding of client assets); EF4 (facilitation of credit creation); and EF5 (securitisation-based credit intermediation).

provisions.title: NBFI Leverage and Liquidity Risks
provisions.text: The FSB has identified leverage and liquidity risks as key vulnerabilities in the NBFI sector. Key concerns include: synthetic leverage in derivatives markets (pension fund LDI strategies, hedge fund basis trades); liquidity transformation in open-ended funds; margin call amplification during stress (cascading margin calls across interconnected markets); and the concentration of NBFI exposures in specific market segments. The FSB recommends enhanced data collection, reporting, and monitoring of NBFI leverage.

provisions.title: Hedge Fund Systemic Risk Assessment
provisions.text: The FSB and IOSCO have assessed systemic risks from hedge fund activities. Key risk channels include: counterparty credit risk (potential losses to prime brokers and derivatives counterparties); market risk (concentrated positions and correlated trading strategies); leverage risk (both financial and synthetic leverage amplifying losses); and operational risk (operational failures at large hedge funds disrupting markets). Regulators should have appropriate data collection to monitor hedge fund leverage, exposures, and interconnectedness.

provisions.title: CCP Interdependencies and Financial Stability
provisions.text: The FSB has assessed interdependencies between CCPs and their clearing members. Key findings include: a small number of clearing members account for a large share of total initial margin at major CCPs; the failure of a major clearing member would simultaneously affect multiple CCPs; CCP stress testing should consider multi-CCP scenarios (simultaneous defaults across CCPs); and supervisory stress tests should assess system-wide margin call dynamics. The FSB recommends enhanced transparency on CCP-clearing member interdependencies.

provisions.title: FSB Global Regulatory Framework for Crypto-Assets
provisions.text: The FSB has published a global regulatory framework for crypto-asset activities. The framework consists of: (1) high-level recommendations for the regulation, supervision, and oversight of crypto-asset activities and markets; and (2) high-level recommendations for the regulation of global stablecoin arrangements. Key principles include: same activity, same risk, same regulation; comprehensive regulation of crypto-asset service providers (CASPs); governance and risk management requirements; data collection and reporting; and international cooperation.

provisions.title: FSB Global Stablecoin Regulation
provisions.text: The FSB has published high-level recommendations for global stablecoin (GSC) arrangements. Key requirements include: GSC arrangements should have appropriate legal basis and governance; GSC arrangements should have comprehensive risk management (reserve management, redemption rights, stabilisation mechanism); GSC arrangements should be subject to prudential requirements proportionate to their risks; systemically important GSC arrangements should meet PFMI standards for FMIs; and GSC arrangements should comply with AML/CFT requirements in all jurisdictions where they operate.

provisions.title: FSB Roadmap for Addressing Climate-Related Financial Risks
provisions.text: The FSB has published a roadmap for addressing climate-related financial risks, coordinated across standard-setting bodies. Key elements include: firm-level disclosures (TCFD framework, now ISSB standards); data and analytical tools (climate scenarios, physical and transition risk metrics); vulnerabilities analysis (supervisory stress testing, cross-sector amplification channels); and regulatory and supervisory approaches (integration of climate risk into microprudential supervision, macroprudential frameworks). The roadmap is updated annually and tracks implementation progress.

provisions.title: FSB Roadmap for Enhancing Cross-Border Payments
provisions.text: The FSB has developed a roadmap, endorsed by the G20, to enhance cross-border payments. The roadmap sets quantitative targets for 2027: cost (no more than 1% for remittances, 1% for other wholesale, 3% for retail); speed (most payments received within one hour, all within one business day); access (all end-users should have access to at least one cross-border payment service); and transparency (all payment service providers should provide minimum service-level information upfront). The roadmap includes 19 building blocks covering payment system interoperability, data standards, and regulatory harmonisation.

provisions.title: FSB Recommendations for Cyber Incident Reporting
provisions.text: The FSB has published recommendations to achieve greater convergence in cyber incident reporting. Key recommendations include: financial authorities should establish cyber incident reporting frameworks; reporting should cover incidents affecting confidentiality, integrity, or availability of information systems; reporting should be timely (initial notification within 24-72 hours of discovery); reporting formats should use common data elements (FIRE format); and information sharing between authorities should be facilitated while protecting sensitive data. The FSB recommends adoption of a common lexicon for cyber incidents.

provisions.title: FSB Third-Party Risk Management and Oversight
provisions.text: The FSB has published a toolkit for financial authorities and financial institutions to manage risks from third-party service relationships. The toolkit covers: identification of critical third-party service providers; due diligence and contracting (service levels, audit rights, data protection, exit strategies); ongoing monitoring and testing; concentration risk assessment (multiple financial institutions relying on the same third party); and incident reporting and business continuity. The toolkit also addresses the need for regulatory oversight of critical third-party providers that serve multiple financial institutions.

provisions.title: FSB Assessment of AI in Financial Services
provisions.text: The FSB has assessed the financial stability implications of artificial intelligence and machine learning in financial services. Key areas of concern include: model risk (lack of explainability, data quality issues, overfitting); concentration risk (reliance on a small number of AI service providers or datasets); market dynamics (herding behaviour from similar AI-driven strategies); operational resilience (dependency on AI systems for critical functions); and consumer protection (algorithmic bias, automated decision-making). The FSB recommends that existing regulatory frameworks should be applied to AI-driven activities on a technology-neutral basis.

provisions.title: FSB IBOR Transition
provisions.text: The FSB coordinated the global transition from interbank offered rates (IBORs) to risk-free rates (RFRs). Key elements include: cessation of LIBOR (GBP, EUR, CHF, JPY settings ceased end-2021; USD settings ceased end-June 2023); transition to alternative reference rates (SOFR for USD, SONIA for GBP, TONA for JPY, SARON for CHF, ESTR for EUR); fallback provisions for legacy contracts (ISDA IBOR Fallbacks Protocol); and spread adjustments to ensure economic equivalence. The FSB continues to monitor transition progress and remaining legacy exposures.

provisions.title: FSB Analysis of Tokenisation in Finance
provisions.text: The FSB has analysed the financial stability implications of tokenisation of assets. Key considerations include: tokenisation of traditional financial assets (bonds, equities, real estate) on distributed ledger technology; the potential for increased market efficiency (faster settlement, fractional ownership, programmability); risks from fragmentation of liquidity across tokenised and non-tokenised markets; legal uncertainties regarding the treatment of tokenised assets under existing frameworks; and operational risks from smart contract vulnerabilities and DLT governance. The FSB recommends that existing regulatory frameworks should apply to tokenised assets on a substance-over-form basis.
